did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically a modified GoogleNet architecture with transfer learning and fine-tuning, to classify skin lesions into eight classes using the ISIC 2019 dataset. It also employed a bootstrap multiclass Support Vector Machine (SVM) for classification in one of the experimental setups.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the modification of a GoogleNet architecture, the application of transfer learning and fine-tuning, and the use of a bootstrap multiclass SVM for classifying skin lesions from the ISIC 2019 dataset. It reports specific performance metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision) derived from these experiments, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin Lesions Classification Into Eight Classes for ISIC 2019 Using Deep Convolutional Neural Network and Transfer Learning
brief_summary: This study proposes a deep learning model for classifying skin lesions into eight classes using the ISIC 2019 dataset to aid in early melanoma detection. The authors modified the GoogleNet architecture by adding filters and replacing the final layers, employing transfer learning and fine-tuning to enhance feature extraction. Two classification strategies were evaluated: one using a SoftMax layer for multi-class output and another using a bootstrap multiclass Support Vector Machine (SVM) to detect unknown outlier images. The primary model achieved a classification accuracy of 94.92%, with sensitivity, specificity, and precision of 79.8%, 97%, and 80.36%, respectively. The results demonstrate that the modified GoogleNet outperforms deeper architectures and effectively handles class imbalance through image augmentation.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the eight classes in the ISIC 2019 dataset used for classification. Described as a type of skin cancer with high mortality rate.

all_task_types.target: Eight classes of skin lesions (Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevus, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesion, Squamous Cell Carcinoma) and an 'Unknown/Outlier' class.
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of skin lesions into eight specific diagnostic categories using a modified GoogleNet architecture with transfer learning and fine-tuning. It also evaluates a secondary classification strategy using a bootstrap multiclass Support Vector Machine (SVM) to detect unknown images (outliers) that do not belong to the eight defined classes. The task is explicitly defined as classifying images into discrete diagnostic categories, with performance measured by accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and precision.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: The International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2019 challenge dataset, which contains images from the HAM10000 and BCN_20000 collections. It is a public benchmark dataset used for classifying skin lesions.

use.training: Used for training the modified GoogleNet model. In Experiment 1, 80% (20,265 images) was used for training. In Experiment 2, the dataset was augmented and split into 80% (24,067 images) for training. In Experiment 3, the dataset was reduced to equal class sizes (total 1,912 images), with 80% (1,530 images) used for training.
use.validation: Used for validation. In Experiment 1, 10% (2,531 images) was used for validation. In Experiment 2, 10% (3,006 images) was used for validation. In Experiment 3, 10% (191 images) was used for validation.
use.testing: Used for testing. In Experiment 1, 10% (2,531 images) was used for testing. In Experiment 2, 10% (3,006 images) was used for testing. In Experiment 3, 10% (191 images) was used for testing. The paper also mentions testing on the ISIC 2019 test set which includes unknown images.
use.other: The dataset comprises images from HAM10000 (~10,000 images, size 600x450) and BCN_20000 (~19,424 images, size 1024x1024).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used to classify images and detect unknown outlier images that do not belong to the eight defined classes.
Used to compute performance measures such as accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, and F1 score for the classification experiments.
Used to visualize and evaluate the performance of the proposed models, particularly regarding true positive rates and false positive rates.
Used to reduce the number of images in larger classes to match the smallest class (Dermatofibroma) in the third experiment, addressing class imbalance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, and F1 score derived from confusion matrices.

ISIC 2019 (Experiment 1: Original Datase.notes: The dataset contained 25,331 images with significant class imbalance (e.g., NV: 12,875, DF: 239). Sensitivity and precision were low due to class imbalance.

ISIC 2019 (Experiment 2: Augmented Datas.notes: Smallest classes (AKIEC, DF, VASC, SCC) were augmented. Total images: 30,079. Performance improved compared to Experiment 1, but bias towards the largest class (NV) remained.

ISIC 2019 (Experiment 3: Balanced Datase.notes: All classes were reduced to the size of the smallest class (DF: 239 images). Total images: 1,912. This experiment yielded the best performance metrics among the three.

ISIC 2019 (Experiment 4: Outlier Detecti.notes: Dataset included 9 classes (8 known + 1 unknown). Total images: 60,162. Used to detect unknown/outlier images. Accuracy was 92.99% in this specific setup.

ISIC 2019 (Experiment 5: Outlier Detecti.notes: Used a conditional rule where images with similarity scores < 0.5 for all eight classes were designated as unknown. Total images: 25,331. Performance measures were lower than previous experiments.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on ImageNet; weights used as initial values and fine-tuned
architecture_used.input_size: Images from HAM10000 (600x450) and BCN_20000 (1024x1024); specific input dimension to the network not explicitly stated but derived from dataset

modifications: Added more filters to each layer to enhance features and reduce noise
Replaced last three layers with a new fully-connected layer, SoftMax layer, and classification output layer for multi-class classification
Alternative setup: Replaced last two layers, keeping original fully-connected layer for feature extraction, followed by a bootstrap multiclass SVM
architecture_details.inception_module_changes: Increased number of convolution filters and width of kernel size; used 1x1 bottleneck convolutional layers to reduce computation
architecture_details.pooling: Global average pooling layer replaced fully-connected layers to reduce parameters

training_details.optimizer: Stochastic Gradient Descent with Momentum (implied by momentum parameter)
training_details.loss_function: Cross-entropy (implied by SoftMax and classification task)
training_details.fine_tuning_strategy: Weights of all layers were fine-tuned (randomly initialized and automatically adjusted)
training_details.framework: MATLAB 2018 with CUDA for GPU implementation
training_details.hardware: Core i7 processor, 16 GB DDRAM, GeForce MX150 NVIDIA graphics card

classification_or_output_head.primary_head: Fully-connected layer (N x 2048) followed by SoftMax layer for 8 classes
classification_or_output_head.alternative_head: Bootstrap multiclass Support Vector Machine (SVM) for outlier detection

preprocessing_techniques: No pre-processing such as noise reduction, segmentation, or enhancement was used in the proposed model.
Image augmentation techniques applied to smallest classes (AKIEC, DF, VASC, SCC) in Experiment 2: vertical and horizontal-shift, vertical and horizontal-flip, and rotation.
Random elimination (reduction) of images in larger classes to match the size of the smallest class (Dermatofibroma) in Experiment 3 to address class imbalance.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the smallest classes (AKIEC, DF, VASC, and SCC) in Experiment 2 to address class imbalance. Techniques included vertical and horizontal shifts, vertical and horizontal flips, and rotation. This increased the image counts for these classes to 1660, 1673, 1518, and 1884 respectively.

Used in Experiment 3 to balance the dataset by reducing the number of images in larger classes to match the size of the smallest class (Dermatofibroma, 239 images). This resulted in a total of 1,912 images (239 per class).

conclusions: The proposed modified GoogleNet model successfully classified skin lesions into eight classes with high accuracy (94.92%), sensitivity (79.8%), specificity (97%), and precision (80.36%) when using a balanced dataset.
The modified GoogleNet architecture, which involves adding more filters to enhance features and reduce noise, achieved higher classification rates than other deeper architectures.
Fine-tuning all layers of the pre-trained GoogleNet model significantly improved classification performance compared to fine-tuning only the last layers.

Class imbalance in the ISIC 2019 dataset negatively impacted sensitivity and precision; however, image augmentation of smaller classes and random elimination to balance class sizes improved performance.
The proposed model can detect unknown outlier images that do not belong to the eight defined classes by utilizing a bootstrap multiclass Support Vector Machine (SVM).
No pre-processing steps such as noise reduction, segmentation, or enhancement were used in the proposed model, demonstrating its effectiveness without such interventions.
Transfer learning with a pre-trained GoogleNet model is an effective approach for classifying skin lesions in the ISIC 2019 challenge, addressing the limitations of limited dataset sizes.
important_remarks: The best performance (94.92% accuracy) was achieved using a highly imbalanced dataset strategy where all classes were reduced to the size of the smallest class (Dermatofibroma, 239 images), resulting in a total test set of only 191 images, which raises concerns about statistical power and generalizability.

The study acknowledges significant class imbalance in the original ISIC 2019 dataset (e.g., Melanocytic Nevus: 12,875 vs. Dermatofibroma: 239), which caused low sensitivity (53.3%) and precision (62.5%) in the initial experiment without balancing.

No external validation was performed; the model was tested on the ISIC 2019 test set, and outlier detection relied on adding external images from reference [49] and healthy skin images, which may not represent real-world clinical distribution.
The paper claims the model does not overfit even with small class sizes, but the third experiment's small sample size (1,912 total images) limits the robustness of this claim.

Outlier detection performance varied significantly between methods: using a 9-class SVM yielded 92.99% accuracy, while using a conditional rule on similarity scores yielded lower performance (81% accuracy, 74% sensitivity).

The authors state that fine-tuning all layers of the pre-trained GoogleNet improved performance compared to fine-tuning only the last layers, but specific comparative metrics for this ablation are not explicitly detailed in the provided text.

The dataset combines images from HAM10000 (600x450) and BCN_20000 (1024x1024), introducing potential resolution bias that was not explicitly normalized or discussed in terms of its impact on feature extraction.
